::: callout-note
# On oil prices inflation transmission mechanisms

![](images/Oil%20price%20and%2010%20year%20nominal%20rate.png)

![](images/Oil%20price%20and%20PPI.png)

What brings consumer inflation is actually monetary policy
:::

::: callout-note
# On firms sentiment on for march 2026

The March 2026 PMI releases from S&P Global, together with the GEP Global Supply Chain Volatility Index, signal a clear turning point in the global macro cycle: a transition from modest, broad-based expansion toward a more fragile regime characterized by weakening demand, intensifying cost pressures, and rising supply-side frictions. The common driver is a negative terms-of-trade shock linked to the Middle East conflict, propagating through energy prices, supply chains, and expectations.

In the eurozone, the loss of momentum is already evident. The Composite PMI declined to 50.7 (from 51.9), a nine-month low and below its historical average, consistent with near-stagnation. More importantly, the composition of growth has deteriorated. Services—previously the main engine—have nearly stalled (50.2), while manufacturing output remains comparatively resilient. This divergence is typical of an early-stage cost shock: manufacturing is temporarily supported by backlogs and inventory dynamics, whereas services respond more immediately to real income compression.

Demand conditions have weakened decisively. New orders declined for the first time since mid-2025, driven by services, while export demand remains soft. Forward-looking indicators reinforce the negative signal: business confidence has dropped to near one-year lows, hiring has stalled, and employment has turned marginally negative. This reflects a standard transmission channel whereby uncertainty and margin compression feed into labor demand, ultimately weakening household income and consumption.

At the same time, price dynamics are moving in the opposite direction. Input cost inflation has surged to a three-year high, with a particularly sharp acceleration in manufacturing and broad-based pressures across services. However, output price inflation has increased only modestly, indicating incomplete pass-through. The result is margin compression—one of the defining features of stagflationary environments—where firms face rising costs but cannot fully transmit them due to weakening demand.

Sectoral data across Europe confirm this pattern of broadening weakness. Only 11 of 19 sectors expanded in March, down from 15 in February, with contractions concentrated in demand-sensitive areas such as tourism, financials, and real estate. At the same time, cost pressures intensified across nearly all sectors, particularly in energy-intensive industries such as chemicals and transportation. The combination of weakening demand and rising input costs is structurally negative for profitability and investment.

The global supply chain data provide the key mechanism linking these developments. The GEP Global Supply Chain Volatility Index rose sharply to 0.57, its highest level in three years, signaling renewed strain. This reflects a classic supply shock: higher energy prices have pushed transportation costs to a four-year high, while maritime disruptions have created shortages and bottlenecks. Crucially, shortages are rising despite weakening demand—clear evidence that inflationary pressures are supply-driven rather than demand-driven.

Firms’ responses are amplifying these dynamics. Across regions, precautionary stockpiling has surged to its highest level in three years, particularly in Europe. While individually rational, this behavior exacerbates aggregate shortages and reinforces price pressures—a coordination problem typical of supply disruptions. At the same time, underlying input demand is weakening globally, especially in Asia, indicating that firms are becoming more cautious about future production.

Regional PMI data show that this shock is global but heterogeneous in transmission. In the United States, activity remains expansionary across most sectors, but momentum is slowing. Growth is increasingly concentrated in supply-linked sectors such as Basic Materials and Industrials, while Consumer Services—most sensitive to real income—has contracted sharply, recording its steepest decline since 2022. Technology has also edged into contraction. This pattern mirrors the eurozone, where demand-sensitive sectors are underperforming.

Asia presents a slightly different configuration, highlighting the supply-side dimension of the shock. While 15 of 18 sectors still report output growth, momentum has weakened broadly, with slower increases in new orders across most industries. However, the Technology Equipment sector stands out, recording its strongest output expansion in over four years and leading both growth and job creation. This suggests that global demand for high-value, technology-intensive goods remains resilient, even as broader demand softens. In contrast, traditional cyclical and commodity-linked sectors—such as Metals & Mining and Construction Materials—are either stagnating or contracting, reflecting both weaker global demand and rising input costs.

The Asian data also confirm the global nature of cost pressures. Input costs have risen across the majority of sectors, with the steepest increases again concentrated in chemicals and energy-intensive industries. Output prices have increased broadly, indicating some pass-through, but the simultaneous slowdown in demand suggests that margin pressures remain significant.

Taken together, these cross-regional patterns point to a synchronized global adjustment driven by a negative supply shock. Energy prices act as the initial impulse, feeding into transportation costs, input prices, and supply chain disruptions. These pressures compress real incomes and weaken demand, particularly in consumption-driven sectors. Firms respond by cutting hiring, reducing investment, and managing inventories defensively, reinforcing the slowdown.

From a macroeconomic perspective, this configuration is characteristic of an early stagflationary phase. Growth is slowing toward stall speed—evident in the eurozone’s implied Q1 GDP growth of around 0.2%—while inflationary pressures are re-accelerating. Importantly, the inflation impulse is not demand-driven but originates from supply constraints, making it more difficult for policymakers to address.

This creates a challenging environment for central banks. In the eurozone, the resurgence of input cost inflation raises the risk of second-round effects, prompting a more hawkish stance from the European Central Bank despite weakening activity. This implies a tightening of financial conditions into a slowdown, increasing downside risks to growth. In the United States, while activity remains more resilient, the sectoral rotation toward weaker consumption suggests similar vulnerabilities.

Looking ahead, the persistence of these dynamics will depend on the evolution of the underlying shock. If energy markets stabilize and supply chains adjust, the current disruption may prove temporary. However, the combination of geopolitical uncertainty, supply chain fragmentation, and precautionary behavior by firms raises the risk of a more prolonged period of weak growth and elevated inflation.

A key transmission channel is expectations. The decline in business confidence across regions is particularly concerning, as it directly affects hiring and investment decisions. Lower employment growth reduces household income, further weakening demand, while reduced investment constrains future supply capacity. This feedback loop can prolong the impact of the initial shock even if conditions stabilize.

In sum, the March 2026 data depict a global economy transitioning into a more fragile equilibrium. The combination of slowing demand, rising costs, and supply disruptions is evident across the eurozone, the United States, and Asia, albeit with regional differences in intensity and sectoral composition. The resilience of technology-related sectors contrasts with the weakness in consumer-facing and energy-intensive industries, highlighting the uneven nature of the adjustment. Overall, risks are tilted toward further growth deceleration alongside persistent inflationary pressures, defining a macro environment that is increasingly consistent with stagflationary dynamics.
:::
